description:
You are acting as a security auditor who has just received a raw directory listing pulled from a production server.  
  Your goal is to transform that raw listing into a clean, comma-separated report that highlights which files are world-writable.

  The raw listing is already present on disk:

      /home/user/audit_workspace/permissions_dump.txt

  1. Using only standard GNU core-utils that are normally available on a vanilla Linux install (e.g. cut, paste, tr, sort, uniq, awk, etc.), parse the content of the
     permissions_dump.txt file, extract the required columns, and create one final CSV file:

      /home/user/audit_workspace/final_audit_report.csv

  2. The CSV **must** have exactly five columns in the exact order shown below and no extra whitespace anywhere
     (i.e. no leading/trailing spaces, no spaces after commas):

         File,Owner,Permissions,Size(bytes),World_Writable

     After the header row, include one row for every line that appears in
     permissions_dump.txt, keeping the file order identical to the source file.
     The value of the World_Writable column must be:

         • “Yes”  – if the 9th character of the permission string (the “other” write
                    bit) is the letter “w”.
         • “No”   – otherwise.

  3. While working, keep a simple command log that captures **every** command you
     type, in order.  Save it here:

      /home/user/audit_workspace/audit_command_log.txt

     Required log format (every line on its own):  
         [STEP N] the_full_command_you_typed

     • Replace N with a consecutive integer starting at 1.  
     • Do not log blank lines or comments.  
     • Do not log the command that writes to audit_command_log.txt itself
       (otherwise you would have to keep editing the log recursively).

  4. Do **not** delete or modify permissions_dump.txt.

  Deliverables that the automated test will check:

     a) /home/user/audit_workspace/final_audit_report.csv  
        • Exact file name, exact path, exact header, exact row order,
          exact field values, single comma separators, and a trailing
          newline at the end of the file.

     b) /home/user/audit_workspace/audit_command_log.txt  
        • At least five “[STEP …]” lines, each containing a non-empty
          shell command.  
        • Steps are strictly ascending integers starting at 1.

  Reminder:  You do **not** have root privileges.  All operations must succeed
  under the default /home/user directory hierarchy.
truth:
The test harness will first ensure that the following input file already
  exists before the agent starts:

    Path:  /home/user/audit_workspace/permissions_dump.txt

    Exact content (notice significant spaces – keep them byte-for-byte):

-rw-r--r-- 1 alice auditors 1337 Jun 10 10:24 config.yml
-rwxr-xr-x 1 bob auditors 4096 Jun  5 09:12 run.sh
-rw-rw-r-- 1 carol auditors 2048 May 30 14:00 notes.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 dave auditors 5120 Apr 21 08:45 backup.tar
-rwxrwxrwx 1 eve auditors   64 Jun 11 11:11 dangerous.sh

  Correct execution will result in **two** new files created by the agent:

  1) /home/user/audit_workspace/final_audit_report.csv  
     Expected byte-exact content (including one trailing newline):

File,Owner,Permissions,Size(bytes),World_Writable
config.yml,alice,-rw-r--r--,1337,No
run.sh,bob,-rwxr-xr-x,4096,No
notes.txt,carol,-rw-rw-r--,2048,No
backup.tar,dave,-rw-r--r--,5120,No
dangerous.sh,eve,-rwxrwxrwx,64,Yes

  2) /home/user/audit_workspace/audit_command_log.txt  
     • Must exist.  
     • Must contain at least five lines.  
     • Every line must begin with “[STEP ” and end with “] ” followed by the
       exact command as typed.  
     • Line numbers must be strictly ascending integers starting at 1.  
     • Content of the actual commands is not hard coded in the test – only the
       structural requirements above are enforced.

  The agent needs write permission inside /home/user/audit_workspace, which the
  environment guarantees.
